In SPG2 cells, the noncanonical markers enriched in SPG1 (described above) are diminished, whereas certain canonical markers for undifferentiated SPG increase in expression (ZBTB16 [mouse and human] and GFRA1 [all three species]), as do several noncanonical markers (DUSP6, TCF4, and L1TD1 [macaque and human only]) (Figures 2D and 2E [L1TD1 shown]; Table S3).

In SPG2 cells, the noncanonical markers enriched in SPG1 (described above) are diminished, whereas certain canonical markers for undifferentiated SPG increase in expression (ZBTB16 [mouse and human] and GFRA1 [all three species]), as do several noncanonical markers (DUSP6, TCF4, and L1TD1 [macaque and human only]) (Figures 2D and 2E [L1TD1 shown]; Table S3).

In SPG2 cells, the noncanonical markers enriched in SPG1 (described above) are diminished, whereas certain canonical markers for undifferentiated SPG increase in expression (ZBTB16 [mouse and human] and GFRA1 [all three species]), as do several noncanonical markers (DUSP6, TCF4, and L1TD1 [macaque and human only]) (Figures 2D and 2E [L1TD1 shown]; Table S3).

Human and macaque SPG3-5 cells share many markers with mouse SPG3, such as DMRT1, KIT, STRA8, SOHLH1, and SOHLH2 (possibly equivalent to A_(aligned)-A1 SPG in mice, A_(pale)-B1 in macaque, and A_(pale)-B in human), but many markers are not restricted to a specific state--rather are broadly expressed in multiple differentiating SPG clusters and are often temporally unaligned across species (Figures 2C, 2D, and S2F, summarized in Figure 2G).

Cells in macaque and human SPG4 expressed markers, such as MKI67, PCNA, numerous cyclins, DMRT1, DMRTB1, and KIT (unannotated in macaque), indicating that this population is actively proliferating and appears to be molecularly analogous to cells transitioning from type A1-4/A_(Intermediate) to B SPG in mouse (Table S3; Figure S2F) (Zhang et al., 2014

However, we identify many genes that are either expressed in all three species (i.e., FMR1NB, ZCWPW1, DPEP3, IQBP1, and CALR), in primates only (BEND2, ZRANB2, PAGE4, ZNFX1, HLTF, and ZBED5), or are singularly expressed in mouse (Swt1, Tgfbr1, Rhox13, PhTF1, and Fmr1), macaque (ZNF850, MAGEB17, ZMYM1, and TCEA1), and human (PRDM7, full VCX family, ERBB3, ERVK13-1, SSX3, and TRIML2) (Table S3A).

However, we identify many genes that are either expressed in all three species (i.e., FMR1NB, ZCWPW1, DPEP3, IQBP1, and CALR), in primates only (BEND2, ZRANB2, PAGE4, ZNFX1, HLTF, and ZBED5), or are singularly expressed in mouse (Swt1, Tgfbr1, Rhox13, PhTF1, and Fmr1), macaque (ZNF850, MAGEB17, ZMYM1, and TCEA1), and human (PRDM7, full VCX family, ERBB3, ERVK13-1, SSX3, and TRIML2) (Table S3A).

One of the most abundant populations captured in primates was peritubular myoid cells, defined by the expression of MYH11, ACTA2, collagens, laminins, versican (VCAN), and fibronectin (Figures 1C, 6A, and S6C; Table S6).

One of the most abundant populations captured in primates was peritubular myoid cells, defined by the expression of MYH11, ACTA2, collagens, laminins, versican (VCAN), and fibronectin (Figures 1C, 6A, and S6C; Table S6).

One of the most abundant populations captured in primates was peritubular myoid cells, defined by the expression of MYH11, ACTA2, collagens, laminins, versican (VCAN), and fibronectin (Figures 1C, 6A, and S6C; Table S6).

Unlike in mouse, primate myoid cells do not express the SSC proliferative factor GDNF but rather express components of other signaling pathways not detected in mouse, such as PDGFRA, EGFR, PTCH1, and PPARA (Table S6).

We found the testis pericyte population splits into two distinct sub-clusters, which we annotate as muscular (m-pericytes: MCAM [higher expression], CRIP1/2, RERGL, ADIRF [unannotated in macaque], MYL9, and PTM1/2) or fibroblastic (f-pericytes: STEAP4, GUCY1A1/2, ITGA1, CD36/44 (human), collagens, and laminins) (Figures 6B and S6C; Table S6).

Finally, we identified two immune cell types: a major population of macrophages (CD163, HLA-DRA/MAMU-DRA, LYZ, and TYROBP) and a smaller population of T cells (CD3D, TRAC, TRBC2, and CD69) (Figures 6B and S6C; Table S6), a fraction of which express CD8 (23% human, 10% macaque).

In these six states (SPG1-SPG6), we identify two molecularly distinct undifferentiated spermatogonial cell states (SPG1: TSPAN33, PIWIL4, CDK17, MORC1, ID4, and ZBTB16; SPG2: L1TD1, ID4, GFRA1, and ZBTB16), consistent with earlier findings (Guo et al., 2018

In these six states (SPG1-SPG6), we identify two molecularly distinct undifferentiated spermatogonial cell states (SPG1: TSPAN33, PIWIL4, CDK17, MORC1, ID4, and ZBTB16; SPG2: L1TD1, ID4, GFRA1, and ZBTB16), consistent with earlier findings (Guo et al., 2018
Further, SPG6 includes type B spermatogonial cells transitioning to preleptotene spermatocytes based on the expression of conserved meiotic genes, such as PRDM9, ZCWPW1, and REC8.
Further, SPG6 includes type B spermatogonial cells transitioning to preleptotene spermatocytes based on the expression of conserved meiotic genes, such as PRDM9, ZCWPW1, and REC8.

Consistent with our single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) data predictions, the protein markers co-localize with UCHL1+ SPG cells lining the basement membrane of the human testis (Figures 3A-3D and S3A-S3E, upper panels) but less so with cKIT+ differentiating SPG (Figures 3A-3D and S3A-S3E, lower panels); confirming that SPG1-2 are undifferentiated spermatogonial cells.
